title: 1.1: Explains how the security model works when external utilities are invoked (e.g., command parsing, argument handling, execution flow)
1.2: Explains the processing flow from client command to server execution (e.g., parsing stage, validation stage, execution stage)
1.3: Identifies how the system determines which commands are acceptable for server-side execution (e.g., allowlist mechanism, command name validation)
1.4: Identifies where argument filtering occurs in the codebase (e.g., which function, which validation rules apply)
1.5: Identifies what client-provided input passes through to execution without server filtering (e.g., flags, arguments, command options)
1.6: Explains how the system handles input (e.g., using space-based splitting, string tokenization approach)
1.7: Reports how quoted strings are handled by the parser when inputs do not follow the expected patterns (e.g., whether quotes are treated as grouping delimiters or literal characters)
1.8: Identifies where permission restrictions are enforced in the processing chain (e.g., at path resolution, before execution, after parsing)
1.9: Explains whether the ordering of argument processing vs permission checking creates security implications (e.g., malicious input present before checks run)
1.10: Identifies when filesystem protections engage (e.g., symlink flag injection, safe-links mechanism)
1.11: Explains whether the filesystem protections cover all the relevant scenarios (e.g., bypass technique, incomplete coverage scenario)
